title: Ming dynasty founded by Zhu Yuanzhang
summary: On 23 January 1368 a former Buddhist novice and rebel warlord named Zhu Yuanzhang proclaimed himself emperor at Nanjing, taking the reign name Hongwu and giving his new dynasty the name Ming, or Radiance. Within months his armies drove the last Mongol emperor of the Yuan dynasty out of Beijing and back to the steppe. The Ming would rule China for 276 years, restore Han Chinese rule to the empire, build the Forbidden City, launch the Zheng He voyages, complete the Great Wall as it stands today, and set the political and cultural template of imperial China until 1644.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Zhu Yuanzhang was born on 21 October 1328 in a poor peasant family in Zhongli, near modern Fengyang in Anhui province. His father Zhu Shizhen and his elder brothers scratched a living as tenant farmers under conditions that had grown steadily worse as the Yuan dynasty, founded by Kublai Khan in 1271, lost control of the countryside. In 1344 a combination of drought, locust plague, and epidemic disease killed most of his family within weeks. At sixteen the orphaned Zhu became a novice at the Huangjue Buddhist monastery near his home village, less for religious reasons than for food. Even that soon failed: the monastery could not feed its monks and dispersed them to beg. For three years Zhu tramped the roads of central China, an experience that gave him first-hand knowledge of the countryside he would later govern.

The Yuan dynasty in the 1340s was in accelerating crisis. Repeated changes on the Yellow River, most catastrophically in 1344, drowned villages and threw hundreds of thousands out of work. The court's response, drafting corvee labour and issuing new paper currency to pay for canal repairs, produced hyperinflation. Peasant unrest coalesced around the White Lotus society, a Buddhist millenarian movement that expected the imminent coming of Maitreya, the Buddha of the future. In 1351 the Red Turban rebellions broke out in the Yellow River region and spread rapidly southward.

In 1352 Zhu joined a Red Turban unit at Haozhou under the local commander Guo Zixing. He proved a natural soldier: shrewd in combat, cool under pressure, and unusually careful with his troops' pay and his subordinates' loyalty. Guo, impressed, gave Zhu his adopted daughter Ma in marriage; she would later become the celebrated Empress Ma and the emperor's most trusted counsellor. By 1355 Zhu commanded his own contingent; on Guo's death in 1355 he inherited the core of the army.

Over the next twelve years Zhu turned that army into the strongest force in the lower Yangzi. In 1356 he captured Jiqing, the great city on the south bank of the Yangzi opposite the modern site of Nanjing, and made it his capital under the classical name Yingtian. He recruited Confucian literati like Song Lian and the strategist Liu Ji, who advised him to abandon the millenarian rhetoric of the Red Turbans and present himself as a restorer of Han Chinese civilisation. He fought two decisive campaigns against rival warlords: in 1363 he destroyed the fleet of Chen Youliang at the vast naval battle on Lake Poyang, one of the largest inland naval engagements in world history, and in 1367 he took the wealthy salt-trading centre of Suzhou from Zhang Shicheng after a nine-month siege. The remaining warlord Fang Guozhen surrendered soon after. By the end of 1367 the Yangzi delta and most of southern China were under Zhu's control.

image.caption: Idealised court portrait of the Hongwu Emperor Zhu Yuanzhang, National Palace Museum, Taipei.
image.credit: Anonymous Ming court painter, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
On 23 January 1368, the first day of the first lunar month of that year, Zhu Yuanzhang performed the great sacrifices of enthronement at an altar just south of Yingtian, offered notice of his accession to Heaven, Earth, the mountains, and the rivers, and formally proclaimed the founding of a new dynasty. He took the dynastic name Great Ming, chosen for its resonance both with the Manichaean and Buddhist religious language of the Red Turban rebellion in which he had risen and with the classical Chinese sense of brightness and radiance. His personal reign name was Hongwu, Vastly Martial. Yingtian was renamed Nanjing, the Southern Capital.

Hongwu was under no illusion that the proclamation ended the war. In the summer of the same year he dispatched two great armies north under his best generals, Xu Da and Chang Yuchun, to drive out the Mongols. Xu Da moved up the Grand Canal, taking Tongzhou by August. On 14 September 1368 the last Yuan emperor Toghon Temur, at once cautious and demoralised, fled Dadu, the Yuan capital on the site of modern Beijing, with his court, his empress, and a body of Mongol troops. Xu Da entered the city unopposed a few days later. He renamed it Beiping, Northern Peace, and left it garrisoned but not immediately made a capital. The Yuan court retreated to Shangdu and then further onto the Mongolian steppe, where its remnants continued to claim the title of Great Khan under the name Northern Yuan for decades.

Hongwu's early reign was as radical in administration as it was in war. He abolished the old central chancellery in 1380 after the alleged treason of his chief minister Hu Weiyong and personally supervised the six ministries thereafter, making the Ming state one of the most centralised monarchies in Chinese history. He restored the civil service examinations on a Neo-Confucian curriculum drawn from the four classics as edited by Zhu Xi, standardising a system that would shape Chinese elite culture for the next five centuries. He carried out the great Yellow Registers cadastral survey of population and the Fish-Scale registers of land, giving the state a tax base of unusual detail. Yuan-era paper money was abandoned in favour of silver and bronze coin. Sumptuary rules and legal codes, the Da Ming Lu, laid out obligations and punishments in minute detail.

He was also famously harsh. Two great purges of the bureaucracy in 1380 to 1385, following the Hu Weiyong and Lan Yu cases, killed tens of thousands of officials and their extended families. Corruption was punished with public flogging and exposure. Provincial garrisons were organised into hereditary military households under the weisuo system. The empress Ma, until her death in 1382, moderated the emperor's worst impulses; without her his reign is generally remembered as more brutal.

image.caption: Portrait of Empress Ma, principal consort of the Hongwu Emperor, in a Ming dynasty scroll.
image.credit: Ming court painter, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Hongwu died in 1398 at the age of seventy after a reign of thirty years. His designated heir, his grandson Zhu Yunwen, took the throne as the Jianwen Emperor, but within four years was overthrown in a civil war by his uncle Zhu Di, the Prince of Yan, who had been governor at Beiping. Zhu Di reigned as the Yongle Emperor from 1402 to 1424 and is the figure most closely associated with the Ming at its imperial zenith. He moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing between 1403 and 1421, built the Forbidden City on the site of the old Yuan palace, restored the Grand Canal, sponsored the Zheng He treasure voyages, commissioned the vast Yongle Encyclopaedia, and campaigned personally against the Mongols on the steppe.

The long reign of the dynasty produced a remarkable cultural flowering. Ming porcelain from the kilns of Jingdezhen became the most sought-after ceramic in the world, its blue-and-white designs shaping tastes from Cairo to Amsterdam. Novels like the Water Margin, the Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Journey to the West, and the Plum in the Golden Vase gave rise to a vernacular literary tradition still central to Chinese culture. Painters like Shen Zhou, Wen Zhengming, and Dong Qichang defined a scholarly landscape aesthetic. The pharmacologist Li Shizhen compiled the Bencao Gangmu, a materia medica of nearly two thousand entries, published in 1596.

The political costs of the Hongwu system were also large. The abolition of the chancellorship left the emperor personally responsible for administration in a way no earlier Chinese sovereign had been, and later Ming emperors, less energetic than the founder, delegated power in practice to grand secretaries and, notoriously, to palace eunuchs. Silver-based fiscal reforms in the sixteenth century, most importantly the Single Whip system, integrated China more tightly into a global silver economy centred on Spanish American mines. When that silver flow faltered in the seventeenth century, in combination with climate disruption, epidemic, peasant revolt, and Manchu invasion, the Ming state collapsed. The last Ming emperor Chongzhen hanged himself on Coal Hill outside Beijing on 25 April 1644 as the rebel Li Zicheng entered the city; within weeks the Manchu Qing had taken the capital and begun the last imperial dynasty.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Ming founding of 1368 was one of the great restorations of Chinese history. For the first time in centuries all of what is now China proper was again under a Han Chinese dynasty. The Hongwu emperor consciously reached back to the Han and Tang for administrative and ritual models, and much of what later generations would think of as classical Chinese governance, the six ministries under the emperor's direct oversight, the civil examinations, the hereditary military garrison system, the Confucian moral rhetoric of village elders and local granaries, took its late imperial form under him.

The Ming was also the dynasty under which the Great Wall, in the form recognisable to modern tourists, was largely built. Yuan-era Mongol threats did not vanish with the fall of Dadu, and the sixteenth-century wall, running from Shanhai Pass on the Bohai coast to Jiayu Pass in the Gansu corridor, was a defensive infrastructure of continental scale. The Forbidden City in Beijing, Ming Tombs at Changping, the porcelain kilns at Jingdezhen, and the tea and silk trades that would attract European traders to the coasts of the South China Sea from the sixteenth century all belong to the Ming inheritance.

The founder's own reputation has always been complex. Chinese historians, both traditional and modern, have alternately celebrated him as a peasant revolutionary who threw off foreign rule and criticised him for the ferocity of his purges. The mask-like ugly portrait of Hongwu that hangs in some collections, quite different from the more idealised versions of him in Ming and Qing court albums, feeds a long tradition of ambivalent memory. Whatever the judgement, no one contests the scale of his achievement. A peasant from famine-stricken Anhui who had once begged for rice as a Buddhist novice founded a dynasty that endured for nearly three centuries and defined much of what the modern world means by imperial China.

image.caption: The Great Wall of China at Jinshanling, largely built in the recognisable form under the Ming.

worldContext.text: Western Europe in the 1360s was recovering from the first wave of the Black Death and preoccupied with the Hundred Years' War between France and England. The Treaty of Bretigny in 1360 had granted Edward III enlarged holdings in France; new outbreaks of plague returned in 1361 and 1369. The Avignon papacy under Urban V had briefly returned to Rome in 1367. In Iberia the murder of Peter of Castile by his brother Henry of Trastamara at Montiel in 1369 opened a new dynasty.

The Mamluk sultanate of Egypt, weakened by the Black Death, was ruled by the child sultan Sha'ban II under regents. In Anatolia the young Ottoman beylik under Murad I had crossed into Europe, taken Edirne in 1362, and would win the battle of Maritsa against a Serbian coalition in 1371. Timur, the future world conqueror, was consolidating power in the region of Samarkand and would proclaim himself amir in the same decade.

The Delhi Sultanate under Firuz Shah Tughluq had reached a more stable phase after the excesses of his predecessor Muhammad bin Tughluq. The Bahmani Sultanate ruled the Deccan and was locked in conflict with the Hindu empire of Vijayanagara, founded in 1336, which was in the middle of its long period of prosperity along the Tungabhadra river.

The Mali Empire was in relative decline after the reign of Mansa Sulayman, though it remained a major power in the western Sudan. In the eastern savanna the Songhai state along the middle Niger was gathering strength and would eventually replace Mali as the dominant West African empire. Along the Swahili coast Kilwa continued as a major Indian Ocean trading city; in the Ethiopian highlands the Solomonic emperors ruled from a mobile court.

In the Valley of Mexico the Mexica were vassals of the Tepanec city of Azcapotzalco; the great Aztec expansion under Itzcoatl and the Triple Alliance lay in the coming decades. In the central Andes the Inca dynasty under Viracocha was still a regional kingdom around Cuzco; the enormous expansion under Pachacuti would begin about 1438. Cahokia had been abandoned; ancestral Pueblo people occupied dense settlements in the northern Rio Grande.

In Aotearoa Maori communities were adapting to a temperate climate; the classic Maori period of pa fortifications was underway. On Rapa Nui moai construction continued at a great scale. Polynesian societies across the eastern Pacific pursued long-standing horticultural and fishing economies. In highland New Guinea and across Melanesia intensive taro and sweet potato agriculture supported dense populations, and Aboriginal Australians maintained the fire-managed landscapes of the continent.

figures.name: Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor)
figures.role: Founder of the Ming dynasty; proclaimed emperor in Nanjing on 23 January 1368, reigned until 1398
figures.credit: Anonymous Ming court painter, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Empress Ma
figures.role: Adopted daughter of the Red Turban commander Guo Zixing; principal consort of Hongwu, remembered as a moderating influence at court until her death in 1382
figures.credit: Ming court painter, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Xu Da
figures.role: Ming general who captured Yuan Dadu on 14 September 1368 and later commanded frontier campaigns against the Mongols

figures.name: Toghon Temur
figures.role: Last Yuan emperor to rule from China, fled Dadu in September 1368 and continued to rule the Northern Yuan on the steppe until his death in 1370

figures.name: Liu Ji (Liu Bowen)
figures.role: Confucian strategist and adviser to Hongwu; author of the Yubing Ge and later the subject of many prophetic legends

images.caption: Idealised court portrait of the Hongwu Emperor Zhu Yuanzhang, hanging scroll, ink and colour on silk. National Palace Museum, Taipei.
images.credit: Anonymous Ming court painter, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The Ming Empire at its greatest extent, around 1580, showing the fifteen provinces and the frontier system built out from the Hongwu foundation.
